CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Boys of the "Pool, here I am again! William is back.

We are at the moment suffering from a sort
of .dull headache as tke result of having spent
dose on an hour glancing over the adjec-
tive bespangled columns of Mr. O’Brien’ss paper.
A course of that paper would be calculated to
Unionist.  Everybody
who is against William appears to be such an adjectived
incompetent or worse that the intelligent New Zea-
lander might conclude that the best policy would be to
facilitate the grip
the day when no Irishman would have any greater re-
g'j)onsibility in life than water drawing and wood hew-
g, and let the rest be done by English, Scotch and
her foreigners! Certainly between the incompetence
of the orthodox Nationalist political party, tho miser-
\ble spirit and comparative impotence of the, Freeman's
;@l-., and -jaligrspreech'ng-’and- hysterics of _Wﬂ\yam

5

which England has on us, and hasten

O’Brien and his paper, we feel sick and disgusted with
all brands of specialised politicians, and we should say
that most common sense Nationalists share our feelings.

We have looked over Mr. O’Brien’s speech that he
made at Cork. It is impossible, or at least unprofitable,
to attempt to reason with a man in hysterics, but one
can reason with people who are not in hysterics about
the man who is. O’Brien hunted Healy out of the
Party ; probably at that time he believed that once Tim
was out of the way all would be well—that is the way
with some men, cverything would be all right only for
something, and no sooner is the something got rid of
than another something appears to thwart them.
O’Brien wants Tim back.  Well, for our part, we
would be glad if Redmond and Healy could strike up
a working agreement, but as for O’Brien the great pity
is that when he resigned he didn’t keep being resigned.
Ie is a nervous attack to the country. About seven
years ago Tim took shape in the heated imagination of
William as ““ a poisoned bullet”’ that had to be ex-
tracted; now in the excited imagination of that
emotional person who, like many another man who can
give trouble, has more money than is good for him, Tim
is a sort of angel of peace who must be restored if
““unity >’ is to be established! We won’t attempt to
argue with emotional persons of that sort. But let us
refer our readers to William’s harangue at Cork. In
the course of his speech as reported Mr. O’Brien referred
as follows to the lately kicked out Councils Bill: —

If the Bill had been offcred as a final settlement every
Nationalist would have spurned it, but, on the contrary, the
Minister who introduced it proclaimed it was to be a mere pre-
liminary experiment in self-government which, according to the
terms of the Bill, was to be revised in five years. If the experi-
ment were worked successfully it could only have been altered in
the direction of complete Gladstonian Home Rule. Inadequate
a3 the measure was, it would have given the representatives of
the people control over three-fourths of the Government of Ire-
land under what would be practically a responsible Irish Ministry
and a separate Treasury, with an income of four millions a year,
and guaranteed a surplus of £650,000 a year, such as many States
in Europe would thank their stars to be guaranteed. Talk of
thieir getting Boer Home Rule as a minimum. Depend upon it if
ever they were to get Boer Home Rule as a minimum, they must
first show Boer common sense as a minimum.

As we advocated the policy of attempting by amend-
ments to make the best of the profiered Councils Bill, we
have a certain amount of sympathy with that pronounce-
ment of Mr. O’Brien. But Mr. O’Brien frantically
wants Tim Healy back. Now, who first went bald-
headed against the Councils Bill?  Well, others may
have gone against it before Mr. Healy for all we know,
but ab a very carly stage Mr. Healy took the field
against it, and the Independent,’””  forgetting
in the excitement of the moment that one man’s
ha’penny was as good as “another man’s ha’penny,
Look strong sides against the Bill also. But in the in-
terosts of * unity '’ Mr. O’Brion wants the poisoned
Dbullet ”’ of seven years ago back to the fold! Now, if
Mr. O’Brien and Mr. Healy had been united at the
time the Councils Bill was introduced, what would have
happened? Would the emotion of Mr. O’Brien have
swopb over the judgment of Mr. Healy, or would Mr.
Healy have looked daggers through his glasses at Mr.
0’Brien, and have once again become a ‘‘ poisoned
bullet””?  Surely the probability is that, had Mr.
0’Brien and Mr. Healy been united at the time of the
Councils Bill, it would have been as the unity of two
fiorce cats whose tails had been tied together by some
mischievous person, But ‘ unity’’ is a good cry, and
William screeches. it. The net result of the screeching
of William, the weakness and narrowness of the
¢ Freoman,”’ and the ineffectiveness of the Irish. Z.Part_s_y,
is that sensible people are sick of specialised politicians.

The Waterford County and City Infirmary have, or
may have, some funds to invest, and a list of securities
n addition or substitution '’ of some securities pre-
gxva,'usly sanctioned was decided upon. We do not know any-
thing of the latter securities, but the new ones comprise

Lendon County Council 3} pex conts. ?

I Was the late Judge Fitzgerald called ‘“ One finger
. Jack ”” or “Two finger Jack’’? In our last issuo we
stated that the former was his nickname. Since then
“ You ask, who is Judge Fitzgerald ? The question is a
pertinent one. Hoe obtained his judgeship owing to the
fact that he was a son of Mr. J. D. Fitzgerald, who was
made a Lord of Appeal, and who was known to his con-
temporaries as ‘ Two finger Jack ’ owing to the fact that
when he rose in the world he differentiated among his
acquaintances according to their social rank and shook
hands with them in accordance with their position in
society. To a lord he gave the whole hand, {o an ordi-
nary gentleman he gave 3 or 4 fingers, as the case might
be, to a common attorney he gave two fingers. ITis son,
the present County Court Judge, was known at the Bar
as ¢ David the Dancer’ owing to his terpsichorean pro-
clivities. Another brother, with some of the father’s
characteristics, is a Land Commissioner. All belong to
the class known as Castle “ Caw ’-tholics. ~ The father
was one of the judges who tried the Fenian leaders in
1866, and won favour by his ¢ conduct’ on the bench
and his ‘firmness’ in sentencing John O’Leary and
other Irishmen.”’

Another correspondent, writing us on the same sub-
ject, says:—‘“You are correct in stating that Judge
Fitzgerald, of Glenaheiry fame, was educated at ‘ tony ’
Oscott. You may not know of the circumstances under
which he was expelled. He was the prime mover among
the boys of a select club * whose raison d’etre was the
“ boycotting * and ‘ ragging’ of such other boys at the
school as were not ‘ born gentlemen.” To the credit of
the authorities at Oscott it must be said that the young
snob was severely caned and then expelled. Iis father,
then an Irish judge, took an action against the College,
and you will find the case given fully in the English
Law Reports of about forty years ago. I forget exact
date.”

There are other inhabitants besides mere human
beings in this country—no, gentle reader, we are not
going to weave a yarn of spooks. The population of

2 Treland is very considerable in so far as population may.
be stated in terms of fowl. 1In 1905 there were
18,549,051 fowls in Ireland. Mr. C. Armstrong, a
poultry expert, recently pointed out that in 1901-2 the
Department started a scheme for establishing egg dis-
tribution stations. In 1904 the fowls sold to English
3 fatteners were worth £625,870, while in 1905 the bill
had increased to £677,435, so, that it will be seen that
the trade in poultry between the two countries is an
enormous one. In 1900 it was possible to buy Trish
chickens at from 1s. to 1s, 6d., but they were of poor
quality.  To-day, however, some of the finest table
. fowls in the world come from Ireland, and this says
much for the way in which the poultry question has
there been taken in hand. To show the quantity of
Trish fowl that reach the fattening districts of England
Mr. Armstrong pointed out that one man in Teathfield
 pays to the railway company from £70 to £100 a month
for carriage alone.

A Dublin correspondent writes to us: —‘I am, by
profession, a dealer in horses and live in this city. I
go. every year to the great October Fair at Ballinasloe.
This year, as usual, I went, and without doubt picked
up a number of very fine specimens. On Monday even-
ing I was a bit ‘baked’ after my work. I remarked
to a local man, ‘Ts there anything ‘“ on ’* this evening
here? Yes,” he replied; “a grand concert.” ‘Let’s
go,” said I, and we went and heard what the enclosed
programme contains. There was not a single Irish item
on that programme, though Ballinasloe boasts of a
flourishing branch of the Gaelic League. Some songs
were vulgar—one contained something about the * differ
between a lady and a hiffer’; another given
as an encore was a rigmarole about ¢ Three nice
voung girls and one young man.” The ‘ comic’ of the
evening gave as an encore—though on the drapery were
the words ‘ No encores’—the immortal comic piece, %
“ Charge of the Light Brigade,” rendered by different v
nationalities. ¢ That’s English, quite English, you 35
know,’” was another response to an encore. Well, Mr.
FEditor, the West must be asleep, else it would not
tolerate such productions to be served up on a stage in =
one of the chief towns of the West. The audience
cheered again and again everything on the programme,
and especially the ‘ comic’ pieces. ITow can the West |
be awakened ? ”’

The address or lecture which Professor Craig delivered
at the opening of the présent session of the Trish Vete-
rinary College reminds, or perhaps for the most part
informs, the public that—

Up till about seven years ago all the veterinarians in Ireland
rocoived their professional education in Great Britain. Although
previous attempts had been made to cause a college to be in-
stituted in this country, they failed until a very great enthusiasm
was infused into the movement, and Mr. John Morley, the Chief Sec-
retary at that time, was approached and converted to the necessity
of a college in this most agricultural country. Then a Royal
Charter was obtained in 18% and a Board of Governors created
under the Crown. A grant of £15,000 was given. The College
was established in 1900, and controlled by the Board of Governors,
under the able presidency of Sir Christopher Nixon, Bart. It is
a very remarkable circumstance that the establishment of a
College in Ireland should be of such recent occurrence. Iveland
depends largely for its prosperity upon agriculture, and its studs,
herds, and flocks require more careful attention in these days of
keen competition in order that they may attain their fullest
development. Tt would, therofore, scem reasonable to suppose
that tho authoritics would sce in the interests of agriculture to %
creato for themselves the voterinarians to whom they should i
entrust the care of their live stock, and would ¢ndeavour to. 3
oncourage investigations, into obscure infectious diseases ram:
pant in various parts of the country in order to obtain a proper
iden of the nature of these discases, their means of spread, and
their methods: of eradication and prevention,

Certainly the man in the street would have thought:
that Irish vets. trained in Ireland ought to have as
groab and as long a tradition behind them as Trish horses.
The prospects before vets. in Ireland appear to be ratker
favoutable, and fathers who are struggling with the
problem what to do with our boys might give the veteri-

of vets., yet_ the health and care of cattle, pigs, sheep,
‘and other live stock are of vast importance to the
_ country.  We have heard talk of a scheme for cstablish-
ing a system of veterinary dispensaries, and on the face
of it a country that depends so largely on its live stock
ought to have all the veterinary facilitiespossible. Pro-
fessor Qx‘alg, having roferred o glanders, anthrax, and
other diseases, said, in the course of his address: —

Other diseases which will require to be taken up and scheduled
< are tuberculosis, contagious abortion, redwater, perhaps John's
lilsealsp. and diseases among horses and cattle due to verminous
AR parasites. The time is now ripe to doal with tuberculosia. Tt is
& disease which is common to both man and animal, and it has
been sufficiently shown that animal tubereulosis has been, and
is constantly being, transmitted to the human subject. It is
doing great damage to the stock of the country, and probably
at least 30 per cent of the milch cows are affccted. The veteri-
nary surgeon is in a position both to control and cradicate this
scourge amoug cattle. No doubt a comsiderable amount of
money will be required for the purpose, but that steps must be
7 taken by the Treasury, and that very shortly, to deal with the
disease is ap) 1t to anyone who has watched recent develop-
ments, for two reasons—(1) It affects the agricultural interests of
the country; (2) It has been proved a very real danger to the
public health. The veterinary stafls of these departments will,
therefore, need further increase in strength.

ok A curious thing is that, though Ireland has only had
a Veterinary College for seven years, other countries are
i buying up the services of some of the men whom Ireland
| has trained. A few months ago we were down the
country with a friend who met a young vet. Our friend
advised the vet. to apply for an appointment under the
Department. Evidently the vet. did so, for we saw
afterwards that he had been offered a post under the
Department, but that he elected to take another offer
which he had from the British Army. Professor Met-
tam stated: ‘“ Five of their recent graduates entered
the army in the year October, 1906, to September, 1907,
and at the last competition for commissions in the army
there were 15 candidates and 5 vacancies, and the 5
vacancies were filled by students who took cither the
whole or part of their training with this College. Dur-
ing the past year they had sent four of their graduates
} away, two into India, one to the Malay Federated
3 States, and another to Rhodesia.”” We would like to
sce all the Irish vets. trained in Ireland staying in Ire-
Jand to look after Irish live stock. Perhaps some of
those who got the posts referred to by Professor Mettam
may have heen Britishers who were trained at the
Dublin College.

Mr. T. W. Russell, though he has, as he said, heard
the motor ’bus, is not convinced that the horse has his
future behind him. But he said: —

What I want to point out to you is this: that Ircland has a
tremendous interest in the live stock of this country, and T am
perfectly certain that thousands and tens of thousands of pounds
are 1ost annually by tle farmers of Ireland fiom the want, first
of all, of veterinary services. There are large tracts of Ircland
wheére there is no such thing as a veterinary officer at all, the
cutside districts, and cattle dic in hundreds of thousands because
no assistance can be procured. It has not been the habit to
study that branch to the same cxtent as the other branches.
Tven if the horse disappears there is no rcason why the vete-
rinary profession should suffer at all; there is a large and im-
portant field outside the domain of horses altogether.

All that I can say about it is this—4nd I say it most emphati-
cally—I have made within the last four months something like
twonty appointments of various kinds—five of them were in the
veterinary service—and I have not appointed a man who Was not
an Irishman. I wish to say this, that I decline altogether to
tic my hands or to tie the hands of the Department, and say
that I won't appoint an Englishman or a Scotchman; I won't tie
my hands in that way. We must get the best service that we
can; but I do say that in a country like this—ileaven knows, I
think there are plenty of people to belittle it unjustly and un-
duly, and I am not going to do it—but in a country like Ireland,
where there is so much to be done, where so many things have
absolutely to be re-made; because that is the fact, and where
this work of re-making things has been going on for some time—
I spoak for mysclf and for the Department, and, whilst not bind-
ing myself and not binding the Department, I say that wherever
weo can get Irishmen for the appointments, not only ought we to
do it, but we will take care that it is done.

That is a frank statement that will meet with general
approval and,i we believe, go to popularise the Depart-
ment in Treland.

Are Stations of the Cross to be had on reasonable
terms and made in Ireland? There are new Stations of
the Cross being put up in Dungannon, and we under-
stand the work is being done by a Munich firm, or if it
is not being done at least negotiations have taken place.
Any of the parishioners at Dungannon may have the
privilege of erecting one station in memory of their
departed friends, at a cost of £19, and to put it on no
higher plane money would be well spent in such work.
But cannot the £19 per station be spent in Ireland and
not sent off to Munich?

The formal opening of the Leinster College of Irish,
52 Upper O’Connell Street, Dublin, will take place on
Friday, 11th October, when Dr. Douglas Hyde will pre-
side, the proceedings commencing at 8 p.m. Many pro-
minent people interested in the educational aspect of the
work of the Gaelic League will also attend. The first
meeting of the classes will be held on Monday, 14th
inst., commencing at 7 p.m. Copies of the Syllabus
containing full information as to classes, fees, certifi-
cates, etc., may be had from the Registrar.

We have received the first number of The Irish
Educational Review,” edited by the Rev. Andrew
Murphy. His Lordship the Bishop of Limerick contri-
butes a viogrous article on the University Question.
Dr. O’Dwyer is rather pessimistic, though trenchant.
As things go, or drift, in the political world at present,
he has no hopes of the University Question being
tackled. - In the course of his article his Lordship says:
—“T am satisfied that, if the Irish Members of Parlia-
ment met the Government’s breach of faith on Home
Rule, and on the University Question, by a stern ar}d
implacable opposition in the House of Commons and in
the constituencies, they would wring from the self-
interest of the Liberals what they never will get from
their friendship or sense of justice, and, \}'hatcvcx- of
this, that the Party by the mere fact of taking up such
an attitude would gather to itself the support of all the
genuine national feeling in the country.”’

Over the initials “ M. O’R.”” familiar to our readers,
Monsignor O’Riordan, the Rector of the Irish College
at Rome, deals with  Some Aspects of Primary Educa-
tion,”” and incidentally with the recent lengthened con-
troversy concluded some weeks back in the LEADER.
Amongst the other contributors to the first number are
Dr. Windle, President of Queen’s College, Cork, and
Dr. Isabella Mulvany, Alexandra School, Dublin.

“Cappa'g 'Oud ” writes us (we wish correspondents
would not write on two sides of the paper) with reference
to'our notes last week on the Railway trouble. He
says:—‘“ If you were a railway porter working from 12
to 15 hours a day for 14s. a week, or a driver or fireman
working the same hours for a little more, I believe you
- would join an organisation if you thought it would
better your condition.”” We should think we would;
we have no objection to any man joining a suitable
union, we think they ought to do so, but as we want
scope to manage our own national affairs we think it a
mistake that Irish workingmen should not manage their
affairs, but rather hitch themselves on to English unions.
No doubt they have representation and a voice in the
management, but so also have our Members of Parlia-
ment a voice in the law-making, yet we are not satisfied,
and rightly so; we want the management of our own
affairs, and we want to run Ireland from an Irish point
of view. Why should not the workingmen organise as
Irish workingmen, and organise and conduct their busi-
ness from an Irish point of view ?

Qur correspondent says:—‘ Now, Mr. Editor, I
understand your point. It is that Irish railwaymen
should form an organisation of their own. I agree, but
what would the result be? I say dismissal similar to

= that, I believe, being meted out to the Dublin

Tramway men. -The Companies say, ‘Oh, we

never punish a man for a thing like that,’

but it looks bad anyhow. I say that had

the Tram men been attached to any other organisation

they would have won and some of them would not have

g been penalised and treated as they were. However, I

am going away from the point. I don’t advocate affi-

liating ,with English Trades Unions, but I do say and

believe that Irish railwaymen have received in benefits,

&c., more than they have paid in contributions. I am

not a railwayman, but I know a little of how things go,

and you would be surprised if you knew some of the con-

ditions under which some of our railwaymen work. You

also in your note refer to the fact that a conference was

beld in Birmingham, where a strike was hatched. I do

f not know of a strike being hatched anywhere as yet, but

you might not be aware that a conference was also held

ey in Edinburgh and another in Moran’s Iotel, Talbot

Street, on 12th July last, so you will see that the rail-

waymen of Ireland got a chance to stand aloof if they

j wished. I hope there will be no strike. I deprecate

2 _ strikes as much as anyone, but I say that men, no matter

RIS what they are, should have the right to combine for

§ ~ their own interest and protection. Mr. Bell does not

want a strike, and no one can say that he does, having

regard to his speeches both in Dublin and everywhere

< else. e has left the issue to the men, and with them

it now lies to go on or go back. If the railwaymen of

this country joined the organisation in larger numbers

than they do, both for their own sakes and that of their

families, success would be certain in case of eventuali-
ties.””

Our correspondent appears to be convinced that the
Irish railwaymen are not capable of national trade self-
government, that they must merge themselves in the
railway workers of Great Britain. That is a very
damaging conviction if our 5:q1‘1~espoudcnb happens to
be, as we assume he is, a political Home Ruler. . The
Unionist position, we take it, is that _Irc;la.ud is far
Detter off as part and parcel of Great Britain, and that
disaster would result were she to become a law-making
entity. We don’t know what punishment, if any, has
been meted out to the Dublin Tramwaymen ; but surely
Dublin Tramwaymen and Cork Tramwaymen and the
employces on all the railways in Ireland are strong
enough if formed into an Irish union, or into a.number
of several unions federated, to play the Trade Union
game at home as Irishmen without being a mere tail
to the British lion. Unfortunately the artisans of
TIreland, though they may for the most part profess to
be Home Rulers, are very much anglicised and United
Kingdomised in their capacity as Trade Unionists.
This scems to us a plain and straightforward pro-
position—If the workingmen want political Home
Rule and say that the country would benefit by it, they
ought to act as Home Rulers themselves in the matters
of their trade organisation and have Irish Trades
Unions for Ireland, and Irish organised labour should
look at its problem from a distinct Trish point of view.

SHIRT-MAKING AND BOOT-MAKING IN CORK.

ESSRS. DWYER & CO., LTD., of Cork, besides
being a great wholesale house in the drapery
and hardware lines, are extensive shirt manufacturers.
When we arrived at the wholesale warehouse, we met
the Dress Buyer busily engaged in selecting pat-
terns of Dripsey Mills ladies’ materials for next season
__he was taking time by the forelock in good earnest.
Tn the wholesale woollen line, Messrs. Dwyer and Co.,
and Messrs. Lyons and Co.—we will deal specially with
the latter in a subsequent issue—have practically wiped
out the Scotch traveller in the South of Ireland; and
there is no reason why drapers in the country towns
should go to Scotland for goods, except that many of
them are practically ‘tied”” houses. What with the
gombeen men in the West and in ‘other places, and
the practically “ tied’’ “retailers dotted all over the
country, Ireland has a big incubus to shake off. The
buyers ab Messrs. Dwyer and Co.’s are, we believe,
anxious to do all they can in reason for Irish manu- -
facture—and probably no body of men can do more

“than the buyers in big houses, particularly if they are

backed up by the assistants; and we have heard of
many. good turns that particular buyers have generously

‘Messrs. Dwyer and Co. started as a wholesale house,

and the line of development, or, at least, ono of the -

. lines of development, pursued by the firm is that they
let manufacture wait on trade—that is to say, they first
made a conneetion with other people’s boots and other
people’s shirts, and then started the manufacture of the
class of goods: that they had made a trade in. There
is. a natural process of evolution in this method of ex-
pansion of productive industry that impresses one; but
of course only some firms are in a position to develop
production in that way, for most producing firms are
producers and nothing more.

A swaggering Yank would have a lot of swagger
knocked out of him in this wholesale house in Cork, with
its thirty telephone instruments, and its ever-revolving
parcel lifter that sends goods unceasingly across from
the old block of building at one side of an intersecting
street to the premises at the other side. Mr. Dwyer
made no. secret about the results of the Industrial Re-
vival movement so far as its effects came within his ex-
perience. In the tweed room—we don’t know if that is
the right name for it, but it was a great wareroom where
all sorts of stuff for men’s suits were stocked—we were
told by the buyer that {Zths of the stock was Irish; 15
years ago, the buyer stated, 1§ ths was ot Irish. There’s
a change for you! During the past five years or so,
things have been going ahead rapidly in the Irish direc-

tion, so far as the woollen industry is concerned. Ladies’ ,

dress materials, thanks largely to the enterprise of the
Dripsey Mills, have shown, so far as Messrs. Dwyer
and Co.’s firm is concerned, a remarkable development
during the course of the present year.

We saw the shirt factory in full swing, or in full speed
ahead stitch, we should rather have said. There
were about two hundred girls working in the factory—
a well-lighted compact place on the one floor. Cer-
tainly the girls whom we have seen working in the
several factories that we visited in Cork are a particu-
larly happy and intelligent-looking collection. A shirt
factory employing two hundred girls is a big affair, but
so heavy are the orders that there is very little time to
make shirts for stock; the ovders keep the factory
going ; indeed, the complaint here, as in many other
places where Irish industry is going strong, is that they
cannot turn out enough. Irish-made shirts from
Messrs. Dwyer and Co.’s factory are sent to the West of
England and to Wales; but the firm, in the present
relative state of supply and demand, cannot afford to
push their market in these places. About fifteen years
ago, the shirt factory—if it could be called a factory
then—commenced with four machines; now, as we have
said, there are about two hundred hands employed, and
the factory has increased by one-third during the last
twelve months. The girls are all on piece work, so
the more they earn the better the firm are pleased.
During the last two years there were only about three
weeks spent in making for stock. We saw a new high-
speed machine at work which makes 3,000 stitches a
minute! The shirts are cut out by machinery, some
of them are made up of Irish material and some of
imported material. Shirts are made to sell retail at
as low as one shilling, and the Irish manufacturers

But it was the Lee Boot Manufacturing Co., Ltd.,

As regards lighting, we may here mention that

The system adopted at the Lee Boot Factory is that
which in America is called the team system—that is,

one goes along with the upper and the other accom-

again when the sole joins the upper. The making of
soles and heels has a goodly portion of the factory under

a factory like this, people would see three hundred Irish
people at work, competing successfully against the
world, even against, American dumps, in the production

A CAPTIOUS CRITIC AT THE MUNSTER FEIS.

| T is one thing to spin words into awe-inspiring sen-

tences, another to criticize. Indeed, it is quite an
«easy thing to speak ez cathedra on most subjects, pro-
vided one’s audience is satisfied to take the assumed
'superiority without inquiry. But, in the ultimate
analysis, criticism must be judged by the same standards
as it demands. The critic who deals with plays differs
in no respects from his brother who deals with books or
pictures. The. public demands of him not perfection,
for that is impossible, but, at least, that he shall bring
knowledge and a spirit of fairness to the discharge of his
duty. He can, moreover, do nothing more than speak
for himself. He is, at his best, a recorder of sensations
peculiar to him.

In the current issue of Tmr LEADER, your Southern
-contributor, ‘“ Lee,’’ holds forth at some length on the
Trish drama in general, and the recent performance of
‘“ Eoghan Ruadh O’Neill ”’ in particular. It is cer-
tainly a case of the captious critic in excelsis. With
infinite labour he lays down a series of propositions,
:some of which are undoubtedly his own, and others of
which, he informs us, are the ‘‘ feelings’’ of Irish Ire-
land concerning Irish drama. He tells us that ‘“the
, , «dramatic writer who deals with history differs nowise
in aim, in material, from him who makes plays out of
his surroundings.”” This, stripped of its preliminary
-garnishings, is the first proposition of which he delivers
himself. I am not surprised that he should have
‘thought; it necessary to protest that there was nothing

-strange about it. Surely the dramatist who deals with
/ history is in a far different position to him who relies

-only on his imagination and knowledge of life? He'is
bound within limits that his brother artist need not
recognise. His material is the souls of men and their
- -actions, as they have been, not as they may be or are.
His aim is to show the: great currents of thought, the
:supreme characters which are the mainsprings of history,
not the small intrigues and passions of individuals.
‘License he has no doubt, license to so present these
things that the laws of dramatic unity and the conven-
+ions of the stage may be complied with. Beyond this,
none. FHis work must be based on history, not fiction,
and if he is to write poetry he must do so without turn-
“ing historic truth into a fairy tale or a ‘ problem play.”’

Which of these two latter alternatives ‘“ Lee ’’ desires I
am not, able to discover. ¥

So much for his own proposition. With the feel-
ings ’* which he ascribes to Irish Ireland I cannot deal
as I would wish because he does not himself seem to be
quite clear on the point. Towards the conclusion of his -
article he writes—‘‘ It is, of course, natural that the
Gaelic League audience should occasionally desire some-
thing different from the drama of peasant life.”” In-
deed, if he had confined himself to such a fair state-
ment of an obvious fact, no one could complain. What
£ do complain of is that he should set up what is evi-
dently a cockshot evolved out of his imagination in
order to amuse himself and impress his readers by de-
molishing it. I have been present at many dramatic
performances in Irish, both in Cork and elsewhere, and
at every Munster Feis drama, yet I have never heard
such criticism of the Irish drama as that which *“ Lee’’
informs us is the ‘‘ feeling ’’ of Ivish Ireland. There is
no desire to banish ‘‘ bawneens,”” but there is a strong
desire to avoid monotony. The opinion has been ex-
pressed, and naturally, as ‘“ Lee”’ himself admits, that
Irish dramatic writers should not always confine them-
selves to peasant life. It is clear that it would be ab-
surd to write drawingroom drama in Irish at present.
There is, therefore, only one other outlet, the great and
tragic past of our country, and it is this outlet the
writers of ‘‘ Boghan Ruadh’’ have sought. How far
have they succeeded? Perhaps in this connection I may .
be allowed to write a few words of criticism on the play
as it appeared to me. I am not a dramatic critic, in
the sense that I have never written a line of dramatic
criticism, but T have seen many plays in many lands
and languages, and nearly all that the Irish and Anglo-
Irish stage have produced. ‘‘ Eoghan Ruadh O’Neill *’
is one of the most interesting plays I have yet seen in
Irish. It had, of course, obvious defects. It was too
long as acted, although I believe much cut down. Its
dramatic construction was, in many respects, too am-
bitious and beyond the resources of local staging, but
this it is hard to avoid in historical drama. ‘‘Lee’
thinks that it is ‘“ not beautiful and very melodra-
matic.”” Melodramatic it was to a small extent, but
melodramatic in the best and healthiest sense. Nothing
has bheen more wanting in all the Irish plays that have
gone before, and, after all, melodrama is a recognised
department of dramatic work. Full of action it was
also, and life, and vigour. This gave it beauty as did
the simplicity of its dialogue. It is unfair and untrue
to write of its ‘‘setting forth such a tragic figure as
¢ Eoghan Ruadh O’Neill’ in a free and easy, boisterous
and boyish fashion.”” Nothing could have been more
impressive than the dignified manner in which the
dramatist portrayed the man, and, let it be added, the
actor presented him to the audience. One felt the
strong patriotism, the religious fervour, the princely
bearing and single-minded purpose of the hero. One
felt, too, that here was one of the many men who would,
if God had willed it, have carried the cause of Dark
Rosaleen to triumph.

So much for the play. The actors, it appears, were
*“ amateurs, some of whom had never acted before.”” I
am not aware that any professional body of Irish-
speaking actors exists, so why this fact should be a sub-
ject for criticism I cannot tell.. Perhaps ““ Lee * would
have had a special professional corps created for the
occasion. But does not such criticism show the captious
critic at his worst? The acting was ‘‘ riotous.” I
should prefer to call it natural. Certainly both de-
clamation and movement were excellent. The mount-
ing was ‘“ elaborate.” T should call it crude in some

| Tespects, certainly not ‘elaborate,” yet wonderfully
.ﬁqd'considering_opporbunity ‘and place. Personally, I
« should prefer a safiron cloth as a background without
* scenery at all, but I am not so silly as to imagine that,
such staging, suitable for a coterie like the English

These things I only mention as ‘‘Lee’’ does inci-
dentally. The play is, in this instance, undoubtedly the
thing. ~ Yet it is easy to damn and dishearten with faint
Ppraise and superior criticism. It is easy to forget the
effort, the all-night work, the hard study that play
must have cost those ¢ amateurs,”” and it is even easiev
to dismiss it all in a deluge of cold water.  Lee”’
mentions that he noted how the country people enjoyed
“An Sprid.”” T am willing to judge this play by that
test. On the second night I had the pleasure of sitting
next a little Irish-speaking boy from Ballymacoda. He
had never seen a play before.” No dramatic critic, cap-
tious or otherwise, was ever so competent to enjoy one.
I shall not easily forget his face or his comments. One
felt that boy could never quite forget how good it was
to know Irish and how great it was to be an Irishman,

and there were many like him in the audience. I wish.

the authors and the actors could have seen him as I did.
They would not regret, had they done so, the labour that
the play had cost them ; most certainly they would not
be troubled by the comments of any captious critic.

INCE the memorable night on which Mr. Synge im-
S parted, through the medium of the National
Theatre Co., the result of his studies of life in the West of
Ireland to a bewildered and ultimately horrified audi-
ence I have not found myself within the hallowed pre-
cincts of the Abbey. Unable to grasp the subtleties
underlying the comedies of the Synge school, and fear-
ing the result of an incautious display of my emotions,
T have heen compelled, when in the mood for entertain-
ment of a dramatic character, to slake my Thespian
thirst at commercial fountains and to substitute Shake-
speare for Synge and George Edwardes for Mr. Yeats.
Time, however, dulls the sense of injury, and learning
that a new author had arisen within the sacred choir T
repaired last week to the Abbey Theatre, having warned
my relatives to seck for me in the dungeons of College St.
if T returned not at the usual hour, as they would no
doubt there find me, expiating my offence against
Liberty of Speech. ;
This precaution proved unnecessary. The curtain
eventually fell on a smiling author, bowing his thanks
to an enthusiastic audience, and the D.M.P. were not
required. ~ Mr. Fitzmaurice, in his pourtrayal of the
Irish peasant is happier in his selection of types than
Mr. Synge, or else life in North Kerry is vastly different
from life on the Mayo coast. At the same time it must
be plainly stated that it was no exalted, high-minded
. people that he put before us, rather the reverse, but if
his characters were sordid and unromantic they were
at any rate human. ‘The Country Dressmaker’’ has
one point in common with most plays of its kind pro-
duced at the. Abbey, it exposes and depicts the foibles
- and worse of Irish peasant folk, their better qualities (if
any) are ignored. 3 et
. It is easy to make oneself misunderstood on this point.
No doubt we are a highly-sensitive people and apt to
lose our tempers when our defects are pointed out to us.

This is bad, éspecially as it is not our faults we blush at,
but the having them pointed out which annoy us. At
the same time, and I believe I have put this view for- *
ward before, it is to be regretted that now and again
abt the Abbey some play does not set bqfore us some
specimens of high-minded, noble-living Trishmen of our
own day, even if it were qnly to provide us with good
examples which we might imitate. 5 5

Apart from this general criticism Mr. Fitzmaurice’s
comedy must be pronounced a great success. The town-
bred man is at some disadvantage in discussing a play of
this character, but it would ccr‘uan'lly appear to he a
very vivid and faithful reproduction of aspects of
country life with which the author is evidently familiar.
The plot is simple, yet for that very reason the skill with
which it is elaborated is all the more remarkable. We
are shown the *‘ Country Dressmaker ” at the end of a
ten yoars’ wait for a lover over the sea, still fortifying
herself by the precepts and examples of penny 119vclct|:es
against the importunities of her mother and neighbours
to marry a rustic swain. Then the lover returns with
almighty dollars and an almightier twang. Notwith-
standing the wiles of his relatives he ultimately wins the
hand of his first love, and the curtain falls on the re-
united couple. 3

Such, briefly, very briefly, is the outline of the story.
It conveys but little idea of the humour with which the
picce abounds, or of the pathos either, for Mr. Fitz-
maurice has not failed to introduce a strain of melan-
choly amid the gayer notés. As a side light on matters
matrimonial in peasant life the play is at once amusing
and instructive.  The sage reflections of the elderly
neighbour on the folly of waiting for an absent lover
when there is a suitor on the spot show no sympathy
with romance, while the blandishments and sarcasms
of the ‘ mountainy ”’ matchmaker, ‘“ a knacky man in
the line of women,”” are based on a very shrewd appre-
ciation of material considerations. The ease, too, with
which this eloquent and versatile advocate abandons
one client for another, favouring the more likely, excites
admiration. Then ‘‘the gorsoon,’’ sitting dumb while
his cause is being pleaded and in the hour of refusal
transferring his affections to another maiden, does not
comply with one’s conception of an ideal lover. Affairs
of the heart in Kerry would appear to be lacking in
poetic qualities.

When we come to the scheming farmer looking for a
wealthy son-in-law, and to his two daughters vieing with
ore another, and with the Dressmaker likewise, to secure a
husband with money, we are still on prosaic ground.
Cupid, when he dons corduroysand a caubeen, acquires &
commercial spirit not commonly attributed to him when
afttired in the negligé costume he usually affects. Still,
in justice to Mr. Fitzmaurice and to inhabitants of ‘‘the
Kingdom,”” it must be admitted that such things as
scheming parents and young ladies, conscious of the
advantages of a “ good establishment,”” have been heard
of in more exalted ranks 6f life and in other places than
the County Kerry.

;

The strong farmer, Michael Clohissey, and his worthy

wife are well drawn characters. Dwellers in cities may
hear with a pang the farmer’s references to ** episodes **
connected with the Land agitation, for ““the hand of
little employment hath the daintier sense,”’ and those
remote from the strife are perchance inclined to think
that all concerned therein were heroes. No doubt a
Clohissey here and there appeared in the ranks, but (to
return for a moment to my previous point) why drama-
tise only the Clohisseys? = The machinations of the
Clohissey family to capture the ‘ returned Yank ’’ are
most dlvgrtmg, and their ultimate failure affords that
gratification to one’s higher feelings so pleasing to all
right-minded play-goers.

HE orchards T wrote of last week as having been
luxuriant in parts of Ireland forty years or so
ago, appear to have been apple orchards solely or nearly
so. A few people here and there grew gooseberries,
but the bushes were so close together that they ran
wild, As for jam or any delicacy of that sort, the
people ““ didn’t know what it was; it wouldn’t go well
with the stirabout.”” And so, when the Department !
set its hand a year or two ago to starting fruit-growing !
as an industry amongst the farmers of the half-score 3
districts selected as pioneer grounds, it had practically i

virgin soil to deal with. Tt was aware of the fact, too,
evidently, and was cautious accordingly. Anyhow, it
commenced operations -with no flourish of trumpets. 3
Quite the contrary. The most strongly worded argu- 1
ment I find in the official leaflet intended to enamour -
the farmers of the new idea, as well as to encourage them
by advice to take it up, is this—‘ The advantages to be
derived from a supply of wholesome fruit as an article
of diet for home use are considerable.’”

“ As an afticle of diet for home use!”” T wonder did
the writer of that leaflet think the Ivish farmer would
put himself much out of his way to plant and grow

did not feel any want of, much less craving for? You
might find him interested in a new seed potato, but I
don’t know of any other edible he would get excited
over for home use. For although he lives as well, and
d.iets himself as generously, perhaps, as he need, he
simply has no stomach for dainties. And so I fancy it
is only after, in the hope of making money out of his
fruit garden, he will have taken properly to the idea of
gardening at all, it is only then he will come by degrees
to care for a bit of dessert now and again after dinner

~ the out-worn gospel of art for art’s sake, and appeal
 expressly to the mass of our people, its comedy and-its
tragedy embodying the joys, the sorrows and the ideals

ws and his ideals—presenting him a compelling pic-

I say ““ideal”” advisedly; for ““realism ' and ‘truth
to nature’’ as artistic doctrines were recognised as un-
fruitful shibboleths ten years before Zola died.  The
aim of popular art in the highest sense of the word * .
‘“ popular " is to represent the man and '.;he nation from
the highest and best point of view. Irish pppular ‘art
will show our people their most ngble_ capabilities, and
give practical examples of the }‘ez_xhsatlon of some of our
important (if minor) ideals, stirring our people to work
towards the effective imitation of such examples.

Among the tlieatrical movements at present working
in Dublin there has been much promise, but, consider-
ing the time they have been in existence, ]iLt.le pe.rform-
ance, No body of national drama has arisen in the
ten years or so during which our poets have occupied
themselves witk: this branch of their universal business.
The Abbey Theatre has definitely repudiated any appeal
to the popular audience. Tt remains to be seen whether
the later movements, arisen out of or alongside of the
Abbey, will recognise that national art, so far from be-
coming cheapened by popularity, grows greater in the
universality of its appeal. ;

Our popular drama will, T think, fall back upon the
original rigidly-drawn distinctions between tragedy and
comedy. It is impossible to say which of these types
will be most richly represented in our coming art, but
I should like to think that we are about to have a
flourishing comedy of Irish life.  For while we are
faimous for our quick sympathy with sorrow, we are
too often accused of being somewhat envious of other
people’s joy; a deep, broad charity is necessary for the
true perception of the comedy of life; it needs a very
loving insight into our national characteristics to see
and to reproduce the minor lights which play upon the
grey life of our people, and all the gentleness and love
wkich sweeten the existence of so many Irish homes,
yet which so seldom finds expression in our literature.

Comedy, also, is more appropriate to the progress and
to the moral health of a people which is inclined to
brood overmuch upon its painful history. Comedy will
scothe the smart of an inflamed national imagination ;
in laughter we shall find the healing of our wounds.
Moreover, while tragedy strains towards the future,
agonising towards the unattainable, comedy plays with
the present, making the best of what beauty and bright-
ness life as it is can afford. Tragedy will exalt our life,
comedy will make it more lovable; it is worth the brain
and the hand, and the life-long travail of a genius, to
make Irish life more lovable.

By this comedy of love I mean the blithe high comedy
of which ‘“ Twelfth Nigkt’’ is the finest example, ex-
cluding farce like Mr. Boyle’s ‘ Eloquent Dempsey,’
excluding satiric comedy like his ‘‘ Building Fund.’”
Perhaps the nearest approach in Ireland to this comedy
of comfort is the same author’s ‘‘ Mineral Workers,’”
with its breezily cheerful last act. It is to be hoped
that Mr. Boyle’s defection from the Abbey Theatre will
not close his career as a dramatist.

In view of the present—somewhat belated—popu-
larity of Mr. Bernard Shaw, I must make it-clear that I
exclude from my definition of bright comedy the intel-
lectualism of the Shaw play—a lightning-like intellec-
tualism which may create scenes of a certain grip, but
which leaves us, after going through his work, without
the faintest sympathy for one in his long line of bril-
liant, soulless men and women. Mr. Shaw studies move-
monts, not men. Iis plays show no knowledge of the
practical working of that institution at which, qua in-
stitution, he is in the habit of hurling sociological sneers
—namely, the home.

This digression leads me to a fertile field for the bud-
ding Irish playwright, the family drama. Here we
have a miliew immediate and intense in its appeal to
every man in an Irish pit, and rich in possibilities of
the most varied handling in the most varied keys. The
middle-class home, witl' its blended lights and shadows
of worry and of delight, the comedy and tragedy of
growth and conflict, the struggle of youth against age,
of glad intemperance against stern sobriety, the revolt
‘of son against father with tke reconciliation of that
struggle and the soothing of the revolt through  the

harity which suffereth all things, and is patient to the
—there is an inexhaustible wealth! Mr. Colum has
ed one small corner of this wide field in ‘“ The Land,”
which remains the most vital of our plays. But ‘ The
i t_;nd“ deals, like all other modern Irisk: drama, only
3 fvxth the peasant farmer; and I should like to insist
~ upon the fact that, even in post-heroic Ireland, there
are other classes besides the peasant farmer and his
'enterpl‘lsmg cousin from America. The suburbs of
Dublin and Belfast, and the modest middle-class resi-
dences of our country towns teem witls potential drama.
I have some hesitation in cxpressing the hope that a
great popular tragic art, expressing the most exalted
ideals of our people, may arise in Ireland. Not that I
do not think we are not ready to sympathise with suf-
fering. ~ We are, for the purposes of appreciating
tragedy, a little too ready to do so. The danger of this
is that it will intensify the inclination of our young
poets to adopt the cult of sorrow which all Irishmen
who have attempted tragedy have, up to the present,
adopted—a cult which rules supreme among the Abbey
dramatists, and has cast its shadow even upon Mr.
Colum’s vigorous and genial work. These dramatists
appear to hold that this worship of pain is the essence
of tragic art. T need only refer them to Shakespeare
and to Sophocles to show them that tragedy is not the
apotheosis, but the purification of pain. Suffering in
itself is not beautiful. It is only as a background to
the glowing beauty and strength of a soul, as an impulse
to the birth and youth and manhood of Lope, uncon-
quered and undismayed in the fierce tempest of human
agony, that the sad darkness becomes an instrument to
heart-uplifting light. This it is that brings the invari-
able comfort to the close of each great tragic passion of
Shakespeare and Sophocles, where no symphony of
sorrow craslies to an end without its final note of hope.
It is only when we come to the rhythmless poet of an
age without faith, without hope, and without charity—
the nineteenth century Ibsen, that we find a drama whick
is one long throb of despair. Ibsen’s work is the fit
expression of the age and the country in wkich he lived.
Nineteenth century Norway was a culture-weary, ‘ fin-
de-siécle * nation. In our country, far from being cul-
ture-weary, we are about to put on a new manhood, and
above all, we have the faith which brings hope. So
that, in our national art, the leading motive will be
one strong exultant chord of comfort. Our art will be
the expression of our high hope—the popularisation of
a policy of self-confidence. It will teach us no longer
in doubtfully reassuring Lalf-truths, that ‘‘ the darkest
hour is just before the dawn,” but that the power of
light is within us, the dawn glows within our own night-
defying smile. M. O'D.

Who and what are those important individuals whom

Another class of ranchers are the erstwhile ‘“big”’
graziers, men who rose into prominence on an izflated
banking system, and famine prices for beef and mutton.
While the good times lasted, these men would stop at
nothing in the way of grazing venture. Their opera-
tions were colossal. Hamlets, farms, allotments, and
houses went down before, and were occupied and turned
into grass by these cattle kings. They got the best
beef and mutton-producing land in the United Kingdom
into their hands, and they worked it under high pressure.
The people were driven off ; their holdings were consoli-
dated into large grazing tracts, and stocked with sheep
and cattle, to be fattened and sold as beef and mutton
te the English and Scottish artisans at famine prices.
But on the advent of foreign meat a violent change
ensued. I remember a conversation T had with a Meath
rancher a few years back which gives an idea of the
altered conditions. I asked was he still attending
Salford market for sale of fat stock. ‘“Ol, no,” said
he, ““T had to give it up; you’d have cattle there, but
a big ship would come in with foreign beasts and dead
meat, and then you’d have no buyers.” In other
words, owing to foreign competition, the people of Man-
chester are now getting meat at a semblance of value
instead of being at the mercy of, and charged famine
prices by, the Irish rancher.

The Meath rancher—erstwkile big grazier—now quits
the fattening of cattle and sheep, and working on a
system of bills, banks, and the balderdash of auctioneers,
lets his land on eleven months’ grazing to owners of’
public-houses, road-makers, and others with a little
money ; and the latter, usually supplemented by bank
advances, is utilised for stocking the grass-land. The
eleven months’ system in Meath is the grazing of two
to three year old oxen, for the most part of inferior
quality, but which, when half fat, are sold at extrava-
gant prices to the English and Scottish farmers for stall-
feeding—-the Englishmen and Scotchmen being com-
pelled to buy them, because they have no other cattle
at command. The Meath rancher who lets the grass,
the publican, road-maker, or other who takes it on the
eleven months’ system, the auctioneer who gets the five
per cent. commission, and in some instances eleven per
cent. discount on advances, all dance; but the cross-
Channel farmers, who give the saucy prices for the Irish
stores, pay the piper—tkey make the big rents of the
Irish ranches.

How little outlay is there amongst all those ranchers,

T suppose, went to moisten the quarter of home-made -

‘the Boyne. I have heard of the eleven months’ M
